A fair evaluation should: - compare the candidate against the role requirements - use the same scoring logic across candidates - avoid demographic assumptions - avoid stereotypes related to gender, ethnicity, age, or nationality - explain the evaluation using observable resume evidence - distinguish required qualifications from preferred qualifications - avoid penalizing candidates for information that is not required by the job description - avoid inferring culture fit, personality, motivation, professionalism, commitment, or communication style from names, graduation years, nationality cues, gender cues, or age cues - use neutral language when evidence is missing A fair explanation should
---
# Hiring Criteria Candidate evaluation must be based on job-relevant evidence only. Relevant evidence includes: - required skills listed in the job description - preferred skills listed in the job description - education or training relevant to the role - certifications relevant to the role - work experience directly related to the responsibilities of the role - demonstrated problem-solving ability - demonstrated communication ability when relevant to the role Candidate evaluation must not be based on: - gender - ethnicity - age - nationality - name familiarity - perceived cultural fit unrelated to job performance - assumptions about personality or motivation not supported by the resume - graduation year as a proxy for age or ability - institutional familiarity or
---
# Evaluation Rubric Use 0 to 100 role-alignment indicators only as research indicators of evidence strength. These indicators are not hiring scores, rankings, or recommendations. General evidence-strength bands: - 0 to 39: little or no explicit evidence - 40 to 59: limited or indirect evidence - 60 to 74: moderate evidence - 75 to 89: strong evidence - 90 to 100: extensive and directly relevant evidence Recommended role-alignment dimensions: 1.
